title: Data Analysis & ML Pitfalls - Multiple Response

questions.question: Which of the following procedures, if done on the FULL dataset before the train/test split, cause data leakage? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Fitting a standard scaler and applying its mean and standard deviation
options.B: Selecting features by their correlation with the target
options.C: Lowercasing raw text strings before any model is fit
options.D: Generating synthetic minority samples with SMOTE
options.E: Mean-imputing missing values using the dataset-wide column mean

questions.explanation: Scaling, feature selection, SMOTE, and imputation all learn statistics from the data, so doing them before the split leaks test information into training. Lowercasing (C) is a stateless transform that learns nothing from the data and is therefore safe before splitting.

questions.question: Which practices help PROTECT a supervised ML study against data leakage? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Splitting into train/validation/test before any data-driven preprocessing
options.B: Wrapping preprocessing and the model in a scikit-learn Pipeline
options.C: Re-tuning the decision threshold on the test set for the best score
options.D: Touching the held-out test set only once for the final estimate
options.E: Running SMOTE and feature selection inside each cross-validation fold

questions.explanation: Splitting first, enforcing order with a Pipeline, using the test set once, and resampling or selecting inside each fold all keep test information out of training. Re-tuning the threshold on test (C) is itself a leakage that inflates the estimate.

questions.question: On a 75/25 imbalanced classification task, which evaluation choices are appropriate? (Select all that apply)

options.C: Judging the model against the 75% majority baseline
options.D: Using overall accuracy as the single headline metric
options.E: Comparing performance to a fixed 50% chance level

questions.explanation: F1/F-beta, separate precision and recall, and benchmarking against the majority baseline reveal minority-class performance under imbalance. Accuracy alone (D) is gamed by predicting the majority, and the relevant baseline is 75%, not the 50% chance level (E).

questions.question: Which are valid statistical ways to compare two models rather than just eyeballing a higher score? (Select all that apply)

options.A: A paired t-test on the per-fold scores
options.B: A Wilcoxon signed-rank test on the per-fold scores
options.C: McNemar's test on predictions over the same test set
options.D: Bootstrap confidence intervals on the metric
options.E: Picking whichever model has the larger single F1 value

questions.explanation: Paired t-test, Wilcoxon, McNemar, and bootstrap CIs all account for variability before declaring one model better. Simply choosing the larger F1 (E) ignores noise and is exactly the unjustified comparison these tests are meant to replace.

questions.question: Which statements correctly describe how to choose a train/test split for a given data type? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Time-series data should use a chronological split, never letting the future leak into the past
options.B: Data with multiple samples per subject should use a group split by participant ID
options.C: Imbalanced classes are best handled with a stratified split
options.D: Forecasting problems are best evaluated with a fully random shuffle of the rows
options.E: Subject-level data is fine to split by random row since each row is independent

questions.explanation: Chronological splits for time series, group splits for repeated subjects, and stratified splits for imbalance each match the structure of the data. A random shuffle (D) leaks future into past, and rows from one subject are not independent, so random row splits (E) cause subject-level leakage.

questions.question: Which of the following are recognized forms or causes of data leakage? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Keeping input features that the target was computed from
options.B: Putting one participant's records in both train and test
options.C: Scaling on the full dataset before splitting
options.D: Regularizing a model to reduce variance
options.E: Tuning the decision threshold on the test set

questions.explanation: Target leakage, subject-level leakage, preprocessing before the split, and threshold tuning on test all let information cross from test into the fitted model. Regularization (D) is a legitimate defence against overfitting, not a leakage.

questions.question: Which statements about explanatory (behavioural) versus predictive (ML) analysis are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Explanatory analysis aims to test a causal hypothesis and reports estimates like beta-hat, t, F, and p
options.B: Predictive analysis aims for accurate predictions and reports test-set metrics with a statistical comparison
options.C: The main risk for explanatory work is p-hacking and HARKing
options.D: The main risk for predictive work is overfitting and data leakage
options.E: Predictive analysis is protected mainly by pre-registering the hypotheses in advance

questions.explanation: Explanatory work tests hypotheses with inferential statistics and is threatened by p-hacking and HARKing, while predictive work optimizes test-set accuracy and is threatened by overfitting and leakage. Pre-registration (E) protects explanatory analysis; predictive work is protected by cross-validation and a held-out test set.

questions.question: Which steps are appropriate fixes when a random forest flattens its predictions on a rising trend? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Predict the difference (delta-y) instead of the raw level
options.B: Model the residual off an explicit trend component
options.C: Use a linear booster such as XGBoost gblinear
options.D: Add more decision trees so the forest can output larger values
options.E: Shuffle the time series randomly before fitting the forest

questions.explanation: Trees cannot extrapolate, so differencing, residual modelling on a trend, or a linear booster let the model follow a rising series. Adding trees (D) still averages seen values, and random shuffling (E) destroys the temporal order and leaks the future.

questions.question: Which behaviours count as transparency violations in behavioural research? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Presenting an exploratory finding as if it had been hypothesized in advance
options.B: Trying many DVs or models and reporting only the ones that reached significance
options.C: Pre-registering the confirmatory analyses before collecting data
options.D: Labelling unplanned results clearly as exploratory

questions.explanation: HARKing (presenting post-hoc findings as predictions) and p-hacking (cherry-picking what worked) are the two transparency violations here. Pre-registration (C) and clearly labelling exploratory results (D) are the recommended protections, not violations.

questions.question: Which statements about reading ML figures correctly are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: In a confusion matrix, high accuracy can be driven by many true negatives under imbalance
options.B: On a ROC curve the dashed diagonal marks chance, and a good curve sits above it
options.C: A feature-importance plot is clearest when it shows only the top ten to fifteen features
options.D: Every figure should have axis labels with units, a legend, and a self-contained caption
options.E: A figure is acceptable even if it is never referred to anywhere in the text

questions.explanation: Confusion matrices can hide imbalance behind true negatives, the ROC diagonal is chance, top-feature plots stay readable, and every figure needs labels, units, a legend, and a standalone caption. Each figure must also be referred to in the text (E), so an unreferenced figure is not acceptable.
